perturbation.original_text: (3) "Product" means any computer or related hardware and peripherals ------- (including hubs, switches and routers or networking hardware) or software products (including networking software products) which CompuCom has the ability to sell.
perturbation.changed_text: (3) "Product" means any tangible good, regardless of its nature, ------- which CompuCom has the ability to sell.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Product' is specific to computer-related items. The modified definition broadens it to 'any tangible good', which can cause confusion and legal issues when the contract refers to specific limitations related to 'Product Business' (which is defined specifically for computers/hardware). This potentially contradicts standard interpretations used in commercial law where 'product' in the context of technology has a more specific meaning.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-105. Goods; "Future" Goods; Lot; Commercial Unit.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC defines goods as things that are movable at the time of identification to the contract for sale. Broadening the definition to 'any tangible good' without considering movability introduces ambiguity and potential conflict with UCC provisions governing sales of goods, especially if real property or other non-movable items are implicitly included.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-105. Definitions: Transferability; "Goods"; "Future" Goods; "Lot"; "Commercial Unit". | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-105. Definitions: Transferability; "Goods"; "Future" Goods; "Lot"; "Commercial Unit". (1) " Goods " means all things (including specially manufactured goods) which are movable at the time of identification to the contract for sale other than the money in which the price is to be paid, investment securities (Article 8) and things in action. "Goods" also includes the unborn young of animals and growing crops and other identified things attached to realty as described in the section on goods to be severed from realty (Section 2-107 ). (2) Goods must be both existing and identified before any interest in them can pass. Goods which are not both existing and identified are " future " goods. A purported present sale of future goods or of any interest therein operates as a contract to sell. (3) There may be a sale of a part interest in existing identified goods. (4) An undivided share in an identified bulk of fungible goods is sufficiently identified to be sold although the quantity of the bulk is not determined. Any agreed proportion of such a bulk or any quantity thereof agreed upon by number, weight or other measure may to the extent of the seller's interest in the bulk be sold to the buyer who then becomes an owner in common. (5) " Lot " means a parcel or a single article which is the subject matter of a separate sale or delivery, whether or not it is sufficient to perform the contract . (6) " Commercial unit " means such a unit of goods as by commercial usage is a single whole for purposes of sale and division of which materially impairs its character or value on the market or in use. A commercial unit may be a single article (as a machine) or a set of articles (as a suite of furniture or an assortment of sizes) or a quantity (as a bale, gross, or carload) or any oth
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Product' to 'any tangible good,' potentially including items that are not 'movable' as required by UCC § 2-105. Snippet 1 defines 'Goods' under the UCC as 'all things... which are movable at the time of identification to the contract for sale'. By including non-movable items within the definition of 'Product', the modified text introduces a conflict with the UCC's definition of 'Goods', especially if those 'tangible goods' become part of a sales contract under the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: (5) "Outsourcing and Professional Services" means (a) consulting, ------------------------------------- system migrations, project management, other services typically referred to as "high-end" services, and (b) outsourcing contracts having a term of more than one year which require pricing be done on a per seat basis. Agreements that consist primarily of lower-end services, including, but not limited to, break/fix, IMAC, warranty and low-end staff augmentation, other than agreements priced on a per-seat basis, are not Outsourcing and Professional Services agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: (5) "Outsourcing and Professional Services" means any service provided by Seller or CompuCom. This includes all service agreements, regardless of pricing or term.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition carefully delineates between high-end and lower-end services and sets a specific term and pricing condition for outsourcing contracts. The modified definition broadens it to 'any service' without these restrictions, creating ambiguity. This could lead to a conflict with how 'services' are defined and regulated under state-specific business and professions codes.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law defines services in various contexts related to business transactions. The original definition aligns with potential limitations on services offered within the scope of the non-compete agreement. The modified text's broad definition could be interpreted to include services that are regulated differently or not intended to be restricted, creating a conflict and uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text defined "Outsourcing and Professional Services" with specific criteria based on the type of service (high-end vs. lower-end) and pricing. The changed text broadens this definition to 'any service,' removing these restrictions. This creates a contradiction because the original text aligns with potential limitations, as stated in the Law Explanation. The modified text's broad definition could be interpreted to include services that are regulated differently or not intended to be restricted, creating a conflict and uncertainty. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction definition, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable. While no specific law snippets were provided, the inconsistency between the original defined scope and the new, all-encompassing definition creates a potential legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the terms "CompuCom's Confidential Information" means proprietary or confidential information concerning the Business including, without limitation, information regarding prices charged for Products, the assets, liabilities, and financial condition of the Business, names and identities of customers and analyses of the amount and types of Products purchased by each such customer.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the terms "CompuCom's Confidential Information" means any information related to CompuCom, regardless of its nature or accessibility to the public.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'CompuCom's Confidential Information' is tied to proprietary business information. The modified definition expands it to 'any information', regardless of public availability. This conflicts with the legal definition of confidential information, which generally requires an element of secrecy and competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement defines trade secrets (a subset of confidential information) as information that derives economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means. The modified text's inclusion of 'any information' contradicts this requirement, as publicly available or easily obtainable information cannot be legally protected as confidential.

perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of 'CompuCom's Confidential Information' to include 'any information,' regardless of its public accessibility. This directly contradicts the legal understanding of confidential information, which requires an element of secrecy and competitive advantage, as highlighted by the Law Explanation referencing the Restatement's definition of trade secrets. The inclusion of publicly available information undermines the core principle that confidential information derives value from not being generally known, thus creating a legal contradiction under the Misaligned Terminology definition. While the scraped law snippets are not directly applicable, the Law Explanation, coupled with the Perturbation Explanation and the Original/Changed text comparison, provides sufficient basis to establish the contradiction. The modified text deviates from the legally recognized definition, potentially making the contract unenforceable regarding the protection of truly confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 6.9 GOVERNING LAW. THE VALIDITY AND INTERPRETATION OF THIS AGREEMENT ------------- AND THE TERMS AND CONDITIONS SET FORTH HEREIN SHALL BE GOVERNED BY AND CONSTRUED IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE APPLICABLE TO CONTRACTS MADE AND TO BE PERFORMED WHOLLY THEREIN, WITHOUT GIVING EFFECT TO ANY PROVISIONS THEREOF RELATING TO CONFLICTS OF LAW.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.9 GOVERNING LAW. This agreement shall be governed by the laws most favorable to CompuCom, as determined by CompuCom in its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies Delaware law. The modified clause introduces subjectivity ('laws most favorable') and unilateral control ('as determined by CompuCom'), conflicting with established principles of contract law regarding choice of law and fairness.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 187 addresses choice of law provisions in contracts, requiring a reasonable relationship to the state whose law is chosen or a reasonable basis for the parties' choice. Allowing one party to unilaterally select the 'most favorable' law without any objective standard undermines the principles of predictability and fairness, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable and leading to legal challenges.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original clause by replacing a specific governing law (Delaware) with a subjective standard ('laws most favorable to CompuCom'). The 'conflict of laws' snippet defines this as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute'. By allowing CompuCom to unilaterally choose the governing law, the clause undermines principles of predictability and fairness. This deviates from established contract law principles, making it likely unenforceable.
